Client 2: Same—just keep it plain English and I’m good.
Advisor: Perfect. Thoma, starting at the top—what do you consider your household gross income for the year, before taxes?
Client 1: Household gross is 89000 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing sources—roughly how does that split between you two?
Client 1: Mine is most of it—salary plus some other stuff. Elizabet has some income too, but smaller.

Advisor: Great. Next bucket: spending. About what do your total monthly expenses run—everything like housing costs, groceries, utilities, insurance, the usual?
Client 2: Ballpark, we’re around thirty-five hundred a month—
Client 1: It’s 3600. Sorry—$3,600 a month is what we’ve been using.

Advisor: No, that’s helpful—thanks for tightening it up. So total monthly expenses are 3600. I’m just noting that down.
Advisor: Within that, I want to separate out debt payments. Across all debts—mortgage, any loans—what do you pay per month total?
Client 1: Total monthly debt payments are 1000.
Advisor: Okay. Is that basically the mortgage plus one other loan, or more than that?
Client 1: Mortgage and one loan, yeah.

Client 1: Mortgage and one loan, yeah.
Advisor: Alright. We’ll come back and list each one in a minute. Before that, let’s talk savings and investments. About how much do you have in investable assets total—so cash, brokerage, retirement accounts, anything you’d invest?
Client 1: Total investable assets are 466250.
Advisor: Thanks. When you say investable, does that include your home value or no?
Client 2: I always get confused there—like the house is an asset, but we can’t really spend it unless we sell it.

Advisor: Great—thank you. That all ties back to the investable total you gave me.
Advisor: Now, separate from investable assets—your home. What’s the current value you’re using for your primary residence?
Client 1: We’re using 317,200 for the house value.
Advisor: Okay. Let’s switch to debts and make sure that 1000 a month is made up of the right pieces. First, the mortgage—what’s the monthly payment and the current balance?
Client 1: Mortgage payment is 750 a month, and the balance is 184,200.

Client 2: The non-mortgage debt outstanding is 4,350.
Advisor: Perfect. And when you think about “loans outstanding” as a total—mortgage plus that other loan—what total number do you want me to use?
Client 2: I’d say it’s around 190k-ish total.
Advisor: Okay—ballpark makes sense. If we want the exact figure you’re using in your notes, what is it?
Client 1: The loans outstanding total is 188,500.

Client 1: We’re using 317,200 for the house value.

Advisor: Hi Thoma, hi Elizabet—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into goals, I want to get the basics right: income, spending, what you’ve got saved, and any debts. Sound okay?
Client 1: Yeah, that’s fine. I’d rather start with the numbers anyway.
Client 2: Same—just keep it plain English and I’m good.
Advisor: Perfect. Thoma, starting at the top—what do you consider your household gross income for the year, before taxes?

Client 1: Yes, number of adults is 2.
Advisor: Great. And marital status—are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Perfect. Next—how you want to work with an advisor. Are you looking for an ongoing relationship where I’m acting as an RIA, meaning a registered investment advisor, versus like a one-time plan?

Advisor: Great. And marital status—are you two married?

Advisor: Got it. And then last one—risk tolerance. Earlier you both used the word conservative; I just want to confirm that’s the label you’d pick today.

Client 1: No, nothing in place right now.
Client 2: Yeah, no protection policies currently.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. So we’ve got income, expenses, assets, liabilities, and no insurance policies currently. Next time we’ll dig into goals and what “conservative” means for you in practice.
Advisor: Before we wrap, I want to make sure the debt picture and the home value are captured cleanly, because those drive a lot of the planning. Thoma, you said you’re using 317,200 for the home value—still the number you want us to stick with?
Client 1: Yes, 317,200 is the house value we’re using.

Client 2: I think it’s like… thirty percent-ish? I’m not totally sure.
Advisor: Yep, that’s the right neighborhood. Thoma, do you have the exact bracket band you want me to use?
Client 1: Yes—32%.

Client 2: Right—yeah, that’s helpful. I always want the “what does it mean month to month” version.

Advisor: Perfect. Thanks, both—this helps me set up the next meeting the right way.
